bucolique', Mélanges P. Boyancé, 173-80.
Putnam's suggestive article treats the elaboration and significance of Tityrus' setting in Eclogue
I. 'For the firstime inancient pastoral poetry two
intellectual worlds are incollision, seen inthe characterization of two shepherds only one of whom is finally settled ina landscape of permanence and
continuity.' (163). 'Two people remain momentarily near each other on the
verge of a scenario landscape which continues for one and isrelinquished by
the other. Tityrus is shielded while Meliboeus isexposed, victim of political
and
ethical
importunities
which
his
fortunate colleague escapes.' (181).
Güstin (17)- treats Eclogue 1,inter alia, during his response to M. Delaunois'
earlier articles. Powell (18) tries to determine meaning and value inEclogue
from the dynamics of its internal dramatic movement; in his view, the
riddles (104-7) have no answers. Currie (19) argues that Eclogue III takes its
rise from two Idylls of Theocritus but that Vergil also intended features in
the Roman or Italian comic tradition to be evoked simultaneously. Mayer
(20) follows Hermann's earlier hypothesis that Publius Nigidius Figulus, the
Roman Neopythagorean, is juxtaposed with the Greek Pythagorean, Conon.
Monteleone surveys the tradition and
the
interpretation of the Messianic
Eclogue. Tůmová (22)
includes Vergil's approach to the Four Ages in his
survey. Sutton, Van Sickle, and Hubbard offer distinct, but complementary
studies of Eclogue VI. Chausserie-Laprée assesses the invocation to Arethusa
(Eclogue X) as the last recreation of the pastoral domain and considers the
complex design of phonic reverberations which are an important structural
element.

J.Chomarat/L 'initiation d'Aristée,' REL 52 (1974) 185-207.
Wilhelm treats Virgil's adoption and expansion of Cicero's metaphor of the
sowing of a republic inDe Re Publica, 2, 5. Vergil redefines the Ciceronian
res publica in terms of empire and describes how to sow a new civilization
that will endure. 'Standing between the future Golden Age predicted inEclogue 4.6 and the principáte of Augustus described inAeneid 6.791-794 as
embodying all the qualities of the Saturnian age, the Saturnia regna are represented in this Geòrgie (II) as being an attainable reality with
the proper
application of cultus. ' Habermann (36) finds Varronian inspiration inGeorgics
II, 276-84 and III, 339-48. Lundström reviews past explications of the prelude to Georgics III (1-48) and offers fresh insights. Miles (38) explores the
significance
of
the Amor passage in the
larger context of the Georgics.

Nethercut assesses Aeneas as a second Memnon, as a hero in the image of Heracles (subduer of the man-eating horses of Diomedes) inconnection with Aeneid I, 749-52'. Both are prototypes of the character which Aeneas reveals later in the epic. Craddock's school text provides an introduction and commentary on selected passages from Aeneid II. Narducci introduces Priam's corpse (Aen. II, 554ff) in her study of Pompey's demise (Lucan, Phars. I, 685-6). Both symbolise the frailty of men and empires. Boyancé (53) suggests that Vergil regarded Atlas as the mythical expression of the celestial axis which assured the cohesion and rotation of the heavens (Aen. IV, 481 f). DuBois treats the 'expulsion' of Dido from the city and Aeneas' departure, purified of passion and desire and freed from the trammels of love. Edgeworth recognises the wife of Hasdrubal (Polybius, 38.20; Appian, VII, 19, 131) as one of the models (with Medea, Ariadne, et al.) for Dido in Aeneid IV. Nussbaum examines the oak simile (Aeneid IV. 437-49) as a sample of Vergilian reticence, of the fusion of the external and internal perspective, with subtle reticent inflexions of empathy. Briggs (59) treats Homeric and Augustan elements in the games to provide historical and ideological perspective. Phillips detects a concern for the hero's private and civic past when Aeneas worships the Lar (=pietas) and Vesta (=securitas). Colucci (61) discounts Servius' equation of Averno with in Avernum infavour of the sense per Avernum, 'by way of Avernus'. Heurgon assigns prime importance to Dido in the celebrated passage (Aeneid VI, 450-76) which recalls the silence of Ajax (Odyssey XI, 541-64). Strieker associates the two doves of Venus (Aeneid VI, 98ff) with Delphi and with Judaic and oriental mythology. Szepes (64) recalls Apollo and Hecate as thyriaioi, protectors of the entry and of the city. Hecate is charged the surveillance of the territory which separates the quick and the dead inthe realm of Orcus; Deiphobe, the Sibyl, is her prophetess and has to deal with the world of the dead. Courcelle notes that Christian literature of late antiquity often painted its demons after the image of Vergil's Allecto. Ogilvie offers useful notes on the epic Etruscans, and Mitchell's numismatic study is a useful illustration of the Trojan legends of Rome. review of readings inEclogue IV. He combines the revised OCT of Sir Roger
Mynors, the variant readings, the Greek versions and (where instructive) the
ancient commentaries.
Highet
is concerned with Aen. VII, 373-405, more
particularly with lines 393 and 395: 'They all left their homes. But others
filled the air with shrieks. The logic is wrong.' Highet suggests that there is
probably a lacuna in the poet's composition rather than inthe manuscript
tradition. Monat (126), with Lactantius and Servius, favours ferrea over terrea. Perret seeks to amend the confusion inthe catalogue by aligning Halaesus
(723-5)
with
Falisci
(695-702),
and
by
retaining Massicus, etc (725-32)
aligned with the proper commander, Messapus (691-4). Pulbrook offers two
emendations in the Georgics text: I, 513: invadunt spatia, and IV, 555:
aspicitur (for aspiciunt). Romanelli's study of interpolations and contradictions inthe Aeneid text is vitally important to future editors.

things. Douglas Bush's earlier article, 'Wordsworth and the Classics', UTQ 2

America and today. Saupe argues that Vergil the humanist has not yet been
compromised
by formal analysis. Spiegelman
offers an analysis of Wordsworth's abbreviated version of Vergil's Aeneid with attention to special problems of translation. Tschiedel underlines Vergilian and Ovidian elements in
